1: “Has not man hard service (ṣābāʾ) on earth?” The term ṣābāʾ denotes military conscription, forced labor, a compulsory term of hard service. Christ came not to be served but to serve (Mark Job 10:45, using the same service-language), entering human ṣābāʾ to redeem those under it. He bore the full weight of mortal toil in order to transform it.
2: Like a slave longing for shadow, a hired worker for his wages. The longing for rest from labor finds its answer in Christ: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matt Job 11:28). Christ is the Sabbath-rest that the slave in v. 2 can never reach by toil alone.
3: So I am allotted months of emptiness, nights of misery. Christ descended into the full emptiness of human experience — the kenōsis of Phil Job 2:7. He did not hover above human misery but was allotted its months as truly as any sufferer.
4: When I lie down: when shall I arise? The long night of suffering that makes morning seem impossible — the two days of the disciples’ grief before the resurrection — is the dark night Job articulates. Easter morning is God’s answer to every sufferer’s “when shall I arise?”
5: Flesh clothed with worms and clods of dirt; skin hardened and breaks out afresh. The bodily degradation Job describes is the condition of mortality that Christ assumes — including the crucified body laid in a tomb. The resurrection transforms this exact flesh (1 Cor Job 15:42–44: sown in corruption, raised in glory).
6: Days swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, consumed without hope. The brevity and hopelessness of mortal life is precisely what Christ enters by the Incarnation — taking up finite, fleeting human days — and redeems by resurrection: his risen life is eternal, lifting human days into something beyond the shuttle’s vanishing.
7: “Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will not again see good.” Job’s prayer for divine remembrance is answered in Christ: God does not forget the dying one (Luke Job 23:42–43, the thief on the cross: “remember me” — “today you will be with me”). The breath of human life is not forgotten by the Creator.
8: The eye of him who sees me will behold me no more; your eyes will be on me but I will be gone. The fear of disappearing from God’s sight is inverted by the resurrection: Christ, raised and ascended, is permanently in the Father’s sight as glorified humanity. Those in Christ share that permanent vision (John Job 17:24).
9: “As the cloud fades and vanishes, so he who goes down to Sheol does not come up.” This is Job’s bleak anthropology of one-way descent. Peter’s Pentecost sermon cites Ps 16:10 precisely to say that Christ was not abandoned to Hades (Acts Job 2:24, 27, 31) — he is the exception to the rule Job articulates. The God of Job’s desperate hope raised Christ, breaking the irreversibility Job feared.
10: He returns no more to his house; his place knows him no more. The finality of death as Job sees it is what the resurrection overturns. Christ returns to his disciples after three days (John Job 20:19) — his place does know him again — inaugurating the pattern for the resurrection of the dead.
11: Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit. The full articulation of anguish — not suppressed, not performative — is what Heb 5:7 says Jesus offered: “In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears.” Christ is the one who speaks in the anguish of his spirit before the Father.
12: Am I the sea or a sea monster that you set a guard over me? The chaotic sea requiring divine restraint (Ps Job 89:9; Job Job 38:8–11) is subdued by Christ who walks on water (Matt Job 14:25) and rebukes the storm (Mark Job 4:39) — the Creator who guards the sea himself enters its chaos.
13: When I say my bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint — then God terrifies me with dreams. The inability to find rest — even sleep violated — is the condition Christ transforms: “he grants sleep to his beloved” (Ps Job 127:2). In Gethsemane, while disciples sleep (Mark Job 14:40–41), Christ watches — reversing the pattern.
14: You scare me with dreams and terrify me with visions. The nocturnal terror Job experiences echoes the anguish of Gethsemane (Luke Job 22:44: “his sweat became like great drops of blood”) — Christ endures the fullest concentration of the weight of sin and judgment in the garden before the cross.
15: My soul chooses strangling and death rather than my bones. The wish for death over endless suffering — a despair Elijah (1 Kgs Job 19:4) and Jonah also voice — is not condemned by Christ but transformed: he himself entered death so that the one who passes through it emerges into life rather than being swallowed by it (John Job 11:25–26).
16: I loathe my life; I would not live forever. Job refuses the pretense that merely continuing to exist is inherently good. Christ does not offer mere life extension — he offers life of a different quality entirely: “I came that they may have life and have it abundantly” (John Job 10:10). The resurrection is not resuscitation but transformation.
17: “What is man, that you make so much of him, and that you set your heart on him?” Job inverts Ps Job 8:4’s wonder into a protest — why does God pay such attention to frail humans? Heb Job 2:6–9 answers by applying the psalm to Jesus: the “man” to whom all things are subjected is Christ, who “for a little while was made lower than the angels” (the Incarnation) and is now “crowned with glory and honor” (the resurrection-ascension). Christ is the true human whom the Father “makes so much of” — and in him, all humanity is elevated.
18: Visit him every morning; test him every moment. The relentless divine attention Job experiences as affliction is transformed in Christ. The Father’s constant attention to the Son is love (John Job 5:20); his raising Jesus every morning (Isa Job 50:4: “morning by morning he wakens my ear”) is the pattern of sustained fellowship that Christ opens to his people.
19: How long will you not look away from me, nor leave me alone until I swallow my saliva? The prayer for God to withdraw his scrutiny echoes the Gethsemane cry: “Remove this cup from me” (Luke Job 22:42). Christ asks for the same relief but ultimately accepts the full weight of divine scrutiny on sin — bearing what crushes so that his people receive the gaze of divine delight.
20: If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of mankind? Job’s question presses the theodicy: human sin as burden to God. The Incarnation is God’s answer: he becomes the Watcher who enters the watched world, bearing in himself the consequence of sin (2 Cor Job 5:21) — the Watcher becomes the watched, the Judge becomes the judged.
21: “Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie in the earth; you will seek me, but I shall not be.” Job’s urgent plea for pardon before death is the central question the cross answers. God does not pardon by overlooking iniquity but by laying it on his Son (Isa Job 53:6; Rom Job 8:3–4). The “taking away” of sin that Job begs for is accomplished at Calvary: “he takes away the sin of the world” (John Job 1:29). Job’s fear of dying unforgiven is the exact anguish Christ’s atoning death addresses.
